title: Greece Civilization_The History of the Peloponnesian War
paragraphs: This was indeed the greatest movement yet known in history, not only among the Hellenes, but throughout much of the barbarian world—and I had almost said, of all humanity. Without commerce, without freedom of communication by land or sea, these people cultivated only what was necessary for survival. Destitute of capital, they never planted their land, fearing that an invader might take it away at any moment. They had no walls to protect themselves, so they relied on chance rather than preparation. As a result, they were content with minimal resources and didn't bother building large cities or striving for greatness. He was born long after the Trojan War. He does not call all of them by that name, nor indeed any of them except the followers of Achilles from Phthiotis, who were the original Hellenes: in his poems they are called Danaans, Argives, and Achaeans.

He did not even use the term "barbarian," probably because the Hellenes had not yet been distinguished from the rest of the world by a single, distinctive label. It appears therefore that the several Hellenic communities, comprising not only those who first acquired the name, city by city, as they came to understand each other, but also those who assumed it afterwards as the name of the whole people, were before the Trojan War prevented by their lack of strength and the absence of mutual intercourse from displaying any collective action. During the purification of Delos by Athens during this war, all graves on the island were exhumed. It was discovered that more than half of their inmates were Carians. They were identified by the style of arms buried with them and by the method of burial, which matched the Carians' current practices.

Pelops, who arrived among a needy population in Asia with great wealth, gained such power that, despite being a stranger, the country was named after him. Fortune then saw fit to significantly increase this power in the hands of his descendants. Mycenae may have been a small city, and many of its contemporary towns may seem insignificant compared to others of their time. However, no one who has carefully observed the site would be justified in dismissing the estimates provided by ancient poets and tradition regarding the size of the armies that once gathered there. The city was not built in a compact form, nor was it adorned with magnificent temples and public edifices. Instead, it was composed of villages, following the old style of Hellas, which would create an impression of inadequacy.

We have therefore no right to be skeptical or content ourselves with merely inspecting a town without considering its power. However, we may safely conclude that the armament in question surpassed all previous efforts, as it fell short of modern standards; if we can also accept the testimony of Homer's poems, which, without allowing for poetic exaggeration, reveal that it was far from equalling ours. Difficulty of subsistence forced the invaders to reduce their army's numbers to a level where they could survive in the country during the conduct of the war. Even after their arrival, a victory must have occurred, for the fortifications of the naval camp could not have been built otherwise. However, there is no evidence that their entire force was used; instead, they appear to have focused on cultivating the Chersonese and engaging in piracy due to a lack of supplies.

Hellas, even after the Trojan War, continued to engage in removal and settlement. This prevented it from attaining the quiet necessary for growth. Twenty years after the capture of Ilium, the modern Boeotians were driven out of Arne by the Thessalians and settled in the present Boeotia, the former Cadmeis. Some had already divided there before joining the expedition to Ilium. The Corinthians are credited with pioneering the modern style of naval architecture. In Hellas, Corinth was the first location where galleys were constructed. One notable example is Ameinocles, a skilled Corinthian shipbuilder who created four vessels for the Samians.

She had consequently great financial resources, as evidenced by the epithet "wealthy" bestowed by the old poets on the place. This enabled her to acquire her navy and put down piracy when sea traffic became more common. By offering a market for both branches of the trade, she acquired all the power that a large revenue affords. The Ionians achieved significant naval power during the reigns of Cyrus, the first Persian king, and his son Cambyses. While engaged in conflict with their former allies, they briefly controlled the Ionian Sea. The Phocaeans, who were founding Marseilles, also defeated the Carthaginians at sea.

And even these, although many generations had passed since the Trojan war, seemed to be composed mainly of old fifty-oar and long-boats, with few galleys among their ranks. For after this, there were no significant naval forces in Hellas until Xerxes' expedition; Aegina, Athens, and other cities may have possessed a few vessels, but they were mostly fifty-oars. The navies of the Hellenes during our period were what I have described.

The power of the Ionians was advancing rapidly when it collided with Persia under King Cyrus. After deposing Croesus and conquering everything between the Halys River and the sea, Cyrus did not stop until he had subjugated all coastal cities. The islands remained to be conquered by Darius and the Phoenician navy. The Battle of Marathon took place several years after the overthrow of the tyrants in Athens. It was a pivotal conflict between the Persian Empire's satraps (governors) from Media (modern-day Iran), known as the Medes, and the Athenian army. Ten years later, the barbarian returned with an army to conquer Hellas. The coalition, having defeated the barbarians, subsequently split into two factions: one consisting of the Hellenes who had rebelled against the king, and the other comprising those who had supported him during the conflict.

So that the entire period from the Median War to this, with some peaceful intervals, was spent by each power in war, either with its rival or with its own revolted allies. This afforded them constant practice in military matters and provided them with experience learned in the school of danger. The conclusions I have drawn from the proofs are reliable. With reference to the speeches in this history, some were delivered before the war began, while others took place during its progression; I heard some myself, obtained others from various sources. In all cases, it was challenging to recall them word for word, so my practice has been to paraphrase speakers' words according to the demands of each occasion, while striving to maintain their overall essence.

The war that followed was sparked by the Athenians and Peloponnesians breaking their 30-year peace treaty, which had been established following the conquest of Euboea. As time passed, the city of Epidamnus grew in size and population. However, its decline began when internal factions arose, allegedly sparked by a war with neighboring barbarian tribes. This led to significant weakening of the city's power. The Thebans and Phliasians were asked for money, the Eleans for shields as well; while Corinth herself furnished thirty ships and three thousand heavy infantry.

If she had any claims to make, they would submit the matter to the arbitration of cities in Peloponnese chosen by mutual agreement, and the colony would remain with the city assigned by the arbitrators. The answer they received from Corinth was that, if they withdrew their fleet and the barbarians from Epidamnus, negotiation might be possible. However, since the town was still under siege, approaching arbitrators was not feasible at that time.
